question: US x Iran ceasefire by April 30?
narrative_context: Iran rejected US ceasefire terms on March 25; counter-demands include Strait of Hormuz sovereignty, reparations, end to all US/Israeli ops (CNBC, March 25 2026)
Iran denied direct US-Iran talks exist; US claims are 'talking to itself' (Al Jazeera, March 25 2026)
Pakistan mediators still attempting to arrange first meeting; no bilateral framework in place (Time, March 25 2026)
War started Feb 28 (31 days ago); formal ceasefire within 60 days with one side in active rejection: <10% base rate (historical precedent on major US conflicts)
thesis: Market at 44% prices in a ceasefire by April 30 (29 days away). Reality: ~8%. Iran's demands (Hormuz sovereignty, reparations, complete regional ops halt) are non-starters. Talks haven't even begun—Pakistan is still arranging a first meeting. Even if talks started today, 29 days is impossibly tight. Base rate for US conflicts: ceasefire within 60 days of rejection is <10%.
x_thread: Polymarket: US-Iran ceasefire by April 30? 44%. Signal estimate: 8%. A 36pp mispricing.
Iran rejected ceasefire terms 3 days ago. Demands: Hormuz sovereignty, reparations, end all regional ops. All non-starters. No talks have begun.
Pakistan still arranging the *first meeting*. 29 days to close. Even war-ending talks typically take months.
Base rate: US conflicts with active rejection from opponent → ceasefire within 60 days = <10%. Market at 44% is pricing fantasy. https://dub.sh/pb-x
telegram_post:
🔴 **US-Iran Ceasefire by April 30? Market: 44% | Reality: 8%**

**The 36pp gap:**

• Market prices ceasefire in 29 days. Iran rejected US terms 72 hours ago with unrealistic counteroffers (Hormuz, reparations, full ops halt).
• No talks have begun. Pakistan mediators are still arranging the first meeting.
• Iran's demands are non-starters for the US—these aren't negotiating positions, they're red lines.
• Base rate: US conflicts with active rejection → ceasefire within 60 days = historically <10%.

**Why markets are wildly overpriced:** 44% assumes talks begin immediately, succeed on impossible terms, and resolve in 4 weeks. The diplomatic reality is rejection + stalled attempts to arrange meetings.

Fundamentals say NO. Bet accordingly.
